food_subgroup: Smoked oily fish served as a Korean main dish with rice and banchan

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301737 covers smoked mackerel generically; no specific entry for Norwegian mackerel smoked over apple and hickory wood exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LN covers smoked mackerel as a category. No specific code for house-smoked Norwegian mackerel with apple/hickory wood exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for smoked mackerel as a food item at this specificity level.

preparation_method: served with steamed rice and banchan side dishes

serving.description: 1 plate (smoked mackerel fillet with rice and side dishes)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving consists of approximately 150 g of smoked mackerel fillet accompanied by approximately 150 g of steamed white rice and approximately 50 g of Korean banchan side dishes, totalling roughly 350 g per plate.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, mackerel, Atlantic, raw (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 15046) and smoked mackerel nutrient profile (USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Atlantic mackerel (raw, NDB 15046) and smoked mackerel (NDB 15048). Hot-smoking concentrates protein and fat per 100 g relative to raw fish due to moisture loss. Values below reflect smoked mackerel per 100 g as reported in USDA SR Legacy for smoked Atlantic mackerel. Rice and banchan side dishes are not included in the per_100g values, which represent the smoked mackerel component only.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 (Atkinson, Foster-Powell, Brand-Miller)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure fish and meat products contain negligible digestible carbohydrate and are assigned a GI of effectively zero by convention. As per Veritas v2 rules for zero-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value). The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) confirm that protein- and fat-dominant foods with zero carbohydrate content do not produce a measurable glycemic response. gi_category is low. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero for the smoked mackerel component.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from curing and smoking process (672 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of smoked mackerel due to its high sodium content (672 mg/100 g), which can contribute significantly to daily sodium intake; a half-fillet serving (75 g) provides approximately 504 mg sodium, approaching one-third of the 1500 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero digestible carbohydrate content and high omega-3 polyunsaturated fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Smoked mackerel contains no digestible carbohydrate and therefore does not raise postprandial blood glucose; its high omega-3 fatty acid content (EPA and DHA) may support insulin sensitivity, making it a suitable protein source for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though the accompanying rice portion should be accounted for in total carbohydrate budgeting.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High omega-3 fatty acids (cardioprotective) offset by elevated sodium from smoking and curing
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Smoked mackerel is rich in long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA, approximately 2.0–2.5 g per 100 g) which are cardioprotective and associated with reduced triglycerides and arrhythmia risk; however, the high sodium content (672 mg/100 g) from the smoking and curing process is a concern for individuals with cardiovascular disease and hypertension, and portion control is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: House-Smoked Norwegian Mackerel is a Korean restaurant dish featuring Atlantic mackerel sourced from Norwegian waters, smoked in-house over a blend of apple and hickory wood to produce a rich, deeply flavoured, moist fillet with a lightly caramelised skin. The smoking process concentrates the fish's natural oils, resulting in a tender, flaky texture with a pronounced smoky-sweet aroma and a savoury, slightly salty finish. It is typically served as a main course in a Korean meal set, accompanied by steamed white rice and an assortment of banchan (Korean side dishes) such as kimchi, seasoned vegetables, and pickled radish. Nutritionally, smoked mackerel is an exceptionally nutrient-dense oily fish: it is high in complete protein (approximately 18.5 g per 100 g), rich in long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), and an outstanding source of vitamin B12 (approximately 12 mcg per 100 g, covering several times the daily requirement), vitamin D (approximately 16 mcg per 100 g), selenium, and niacin. It contains zero digestible carbohydrate, making it glycemically inert. The primary clinical concern is its elevated sodium content (approximately 672 mg per 100 g) resulting from the curing and smoking process, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, smoked mackerel is an excellent protein and healthy-fat source that does not contribute to postprandial glycaemia. This dish is served at Korean restaurants in Singapore, including Ajumma's at Funan, and is priced at approximately SGD 13.90 per serving.
common_user_queries: How much omega-3 is in smoked mackerel?
Is smoked mackerel high in sodium?
Is smoked mackerel good for heart health?
meal_context: Korean set meal with rice and banchan
Oily fish serving for omega-3 intake

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for smoked Atlantic mackerel (NDB 15048), which is the closest approved-source match. The dish is a restaurant-prepared item with house-smoking, so exact nutrient values will vary by preparation. No Singapore-specific HPB Foodo entry exists for this dish. Confidence is medium rather than high because the source match is adapted rather than exact.
notes: 1. carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero: smoked mackerel is a pure fish product containing no digestible carbohydrate; this is naturally zero per USDA SR Legacy NDB 15048. sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as fish contains no dietary fibre. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for smoked mackerel. 2. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as smoked mackerel contains no digestible carbohydrate and produces no measurable glycemic response; gi_category is low by convention per University of Sydney GI Database guidance for zero-carbohydrate foods and Veritas v2 rules for zero-carbohydrate foods. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero. 3. Per_100g values represent the smoked mackerel fillet only, not the full plate including rice and banchan. 4. Sodium value (672 mg/100 g) reflects the curing and smoking process and is sourced from USDA SR Legacy NDB 15048. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA SR Legacy NDB 15048 for smoked Atlantic mackerel. 6. This dish is served at Ajumma's - Funan, Singapore, sourced from field survey data at SGD 13.90. 7. Halal status is assumed based on the nature of the ingredients (fish, wood smoke, salt) but has not been independently verified for this specific restaurant preparation.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full record from USDA SR Legacy NDB 15048 (smoked Atlantic mackerel). Added Korean local name and aliases. Documented zero-carbohydrate justification for carbohydrate_g, sugar_g, dietary_fibre_g, vitamin_c_mg, trans_fat_g. Set GI to 0 with low category and 0.0 GL. Added all three health context conditions with clinical specificity. Wrote 200+ character LLM description.

Iteration 2: Addressed validator feedback for q4_glycemic_completeness. Changed glycemic_index from 0 to 1 as required by the 'Zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule' for zero-carbohydrate foods. Updated gi_source.source_match_notes and notes field to reflect this change and explicitly state GI is set to 1. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list. Confirmed all Korean and romanized strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in aliases. Confirmed ontology match_type values use only allowed strings (closest_parent, none). Confirmed all -1 sentinel values are replaced with actual numeric values from USDA SR Legacy where available. Confirmed notes document all zero values with appropriate justification phrases.
